speaker: interviewer
text: And it's the interview with Peter Fischl, and I'm Trish Degennaro, the interviewer. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And the camera woman is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Allison Hoffman.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: The superwoman. [LAUGHS] Peter, is there anything that you'd like to add that I didn't ask about during the war and the pre-war period?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if you didn't ask or we didn't talk about it, but I just would like to emphasize that the world should start to think of loving each other instead of hating each other in order to survive. And not to be prejudice against anybody for any reason. And I think that what, that what I can say. And probably the best thing would be for that education, if, if people would educate themselves. Because I can't imagine myself that any intelligent, creative person would be prejudice. That's what I think I like to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you're, you're a creative person yourself. Tell us about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: About you in America-- you-- when you arrived here in California.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first I arrived back east, and then I came to California. And here in California, I went to college to study advertising and printing. And I worked 25 years in printing, but I think the way I was brought up, the people I was surrounded with, creative people, challenging things I done as a kid and so on, and the conversations I heard in my home as a young kid I think made me a creative person to begin with.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in my whole life I always worked with creative things and ideas. I invented things. 15 years ago, I invented a little electric car, a concept I thought it will be great to have a little electric car which is smog free. And you could noiselessly go around in your neighborhood and drive it. And I made that car.

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of the things I-- the difference between me and probably some other people is that, that I don't just dream about the things, I do it. Although I am not a wealthy person, but somehow I find ways to, to, to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I made this little electric car, and one of the, one of the happiest moments in my life was when I was sitting in this little electric car and put in my favorite music, and it is absolutely noiseless car. I was driving around in my neighborhood listening to the music, the car I created kind of. So they were great moments. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I made other number, number of interesting things. At one time I had-- this was all on the side-- I had a little etching workshop. We made etchings and prints and artworks. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: But I'd like to mention I met a friend of mine about 30 years ago. At that time, he was an artist who just worked as an artist, $4 an hour and done art. And his name is Ralph McQuarrie. And super creative person. And many of the projects I'd done in my life here in California, I done it with him. The electric car also I done him-- with him. He designed the vehicle, the body of the vehicle and so on. Now this friend of mine, Ralph, who lived on Curzon Street, one day he had a visitor named George Lucas walk up into his apartment who heard about Ralph and his talents and hired him. And Ralph McQuarrie became George Lucas's first employee who then finally ended up making movie for him. And so I think these kind of friends also help me in my creativity. I was able not just dream about things but do them because I had a lot of friends with all different kinds of talents who helped me to do things. Otherwise, I couldn't have done it financially, because I couldn't hire anybody like that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One of, one of the-- one of the things which happened to me, which became extremely important in my life happened by chance. I walked on Hollywood Boulevard on the 1960s and walked into a bookshop and found an old magazine, which showed-- which is-- even then it was a famous picture. But now it's even more famous-- about the 1943 Warsaw ghetto uprising. Shows that picture with Jurgen Stroop Dan, who was an SS captain, and who had a newsletter, which purpose was to please Adolf Hitler of how they were taking care of the Jewish problem. And his photographers done this picture of that little Polish boy standing with his arms are up as this brute German with a machine gun standing there, and this little helpless little boy. And this picture had tremendous impact in my-- in my life. And for the next four or five years, there was no day going by that I wouldn't think of that picture in my mind and thinking about it. And I had no particular plan of doing anything about this picture, but it-- this little picture became so much me that I-- actually I talked to this little Polish boy in my mind. And I told him that he's safe now. And I remember these kind of things almost like I would be insane. Maybe I was or maybe I am. But-- so this what was happening, but nothing else. And about four or five years later, what happened in reality, I woke up about 2 o'clock in the morning, and I went out to my living room. And I had a typewriter there and I typed out a poem about the little Polish boy standing with his arms up. Because I'm a straightforward person, I titled it that, because that's what it's about, the little-- 'To the Little Polish Boy Standing with his Arms Up.' And I wrote my feelings down. And then next day, I thought about it for a few minutes, and I thought this is an exploitation of the Warsaw ghetto uprising. Because this picture was taken July 13, after the Warsaw ghetto was taken over by the Germans. They took care of the uprising. And many of the Jews was killed and everything. And I said, I'm not ever going to publish this because this is an exploitation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I put this poem into my drawer, and for the next 25 years or so, it was in my drawer. And just very, very few people I ever showed my poetry. Only one thing I done. Maybe 22 or 23 years after I wrote it, I gave Xerox copies to the archives of the Simon Wiesenthal Center of my poem and they filed it there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we went to a movie and saw Mr. Steven Spielberg's movie I went to see it with my daughter. And it had such an effect on me that I couldn't get up from my chair. And for probably 5 or 10 minutes I was sitting there. And at that very second, I, I knew it, that I will, I will-- I have to publish my poem. And so in about three or four weeks, I published it. And then the Museum of Tolerance exhibited my poem in the museum. And it was an unbelievable success. And then I sent some poems to the United States Holocaust Memorial Museum in Washington, DC, and it was an unbelievable success there. And now there is in several other museums like the New York State museum in Albany, New York, and in Berkeley, the Jewish Museum, and in Hungary, the Jewish Museum. And some other places. And this is a fantastic feeling for me, is-- is because this poem is not just about the little Polish boy standing with his arms up and, and the Germans, what are they doing to the Jewish people. But it's against prejudice. [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] It's against prejudice and, and stop prejudice. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So I wrote this caption that said, about my poem, what I have written about my poem posted, because it is a poster kind of poem. I have-- I wrote, I wrote that when the little Polish boy enters your home, your home becomes a museum. And he will speak against prejudice forever. And I believe in that. And that's, that's, that's why it excites me. And with your permission, I will read this poem to you. Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: The title is-- well, under the picture it says. 'A roundup of Jews in the Warsaw ghetto, 1943.' And then the title is, 'To the Little Polish Boy Standing with his Arms Up, ' by Peter L. Fischl. 'I would like to be an artist so I could make a painting of you, little Polish boy, standing with your little hat on your head, the Star of David on your coat. Standing in the ghetto with your arms up as many Nazi machine guns pointing at you. I would make a monument of you and the world who said nothing. I would like to be a composer so I could write a concerto of you, little Polish boy, standing with your little hat on your head, the Star of David on your coat. Standing in the ghetto with your arms up as many Nazi machine guns pointing at you. I would write a concerto of you and the world who said nothing. I am not an artist, but my mind had painted a painting of you. 10 million miles high is a painting so the whole universe can see you now, little Polish boy standing with your little hat on your head, the Star of David on your coat. Standing in the ghetto with your arms up as many Nazi machine guns pointing at you, and the world who said nothing. I will make this painting so bright that it will blind the eyes of the world who saw nothing. 10 billion miles high will be the monument so the whole world, so the whole universe can remember of you, little Polish boy, standing with your little hat on your head, the Star of David on your coat, standing in the ghetto with your arms up as many Nazi machine guns pointing at you. And the monument will tremble so the blind world now will know what fear is in the darkness. The world who saw nothing, who said nothing. I am not a composer, but I will write a composition for 5 trillion trumpets so it will blast the ear drums of this world, the world who heard nothing. I am sorry that it was you and not me.' [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is the end of, of the poem. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm very happy that I wrote this, and since I published it, I would say thousands of people read it. My, my biggest happiness comes when, when I give the poem away in the post office and I find out that the postal workers have hung my poem in-- into they-- their lunchroom. And then it's there every day and people read it and spread the message.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm very much rewarded. A young lady, a university student, called me up from Washington, DC, and spoke to me for about 90 minutes, that she saw my poem at Washington, DC, in the museum and, and how much, how much she was under-- how much, how much this poem grabbed her. And, and I'm extremely happy that she had a tremendous amount of people who, who have these feelings are not-- non-Jewish people. And who, who will help a to come this world to a better understanding and help for this world to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Peter, do you want to-- do you want to add anything else? You have children, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have a daughter, Cathy, 19 years old, from my second marriage whom I love a lot. And I brought her up after our divorce. She lived with me most of the time. And the only reason she couldn't be here because she is sick tonight. And I have a son [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in Northern California, Tibor, who is about 40 years old and has three kids, a, a daughter and two sons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a message you want to tell the children of this world or--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I would say to educate themselves. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Love your parents and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and, and don't look in happiness like something which has to come overnight. You have to work for everything. Don't look for the easy way out. Don't think that if you take drugs or anything that that will help you or-- The only thing will help in this life what you put in yourself. If you-- if you are a good person and you're good towards others, I believe it will come back somehow towards you. And then-- I, I, I very strongly believe in education. And, and, and don't think of the other as a fool just-- he's a fellow man. And love everybody. Love everybody. Love everybody. That's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Peter, you are going to show us your pictures now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Certainly. Certainly.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I have seen this photograph, as I mentioned before, in magazine in 1960 on Hollywood Boulevard. And then the picture started living with me, and then I wrote a poem. And then after I saw then I decided I'm gonna publish this. Then the problem came up as-- Well, first of all I needed the photograph. How will I get the photograph, and how we going to do it? And so on and so on. So I went to the Wiesenthal Center in Los Angeles to get the photograph. And here came my biggest surprise and shock, that the little Polish boy did not wear the star of David on the photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: What real-- happened in reality, that I was carrying this picture in my mind. And the original magazine, what I had in my hand, had a little light spot at the Star of David, but not really a star. And then-- but my mind interpreted this as a Star of David. So I wrote the poem with the Star of David in my mind. But in reality, there was no Star of David.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I worked on this project with my very good friend, Oscar-winning Ralph McQuarrie, who worked a lot for Mr. Lucas making and worked with Spielberg a lot making a lot of spaceships for him and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I ask him to help me with this. So he air-brushed the, the, the Star of David on, on this coat. Then he helped me-- we asked the paper company to send us samples. And they sent us about 600 paper samples for this poster. And then Ralph helped me to select the paper. In a few weeks, he went through stock and then decided on the ink. And that's how-- and made the final layout that we're going to show it in French and English. That was also an important decision. And then we published the poem poster. 'To the Little Polish Boy Standing with His Arms Up.' [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] They-- we had to make a lot of decisions when, when we made this poem. And there was a lot of pro and cons. I was very strongly for it that we, we will-- I forgot to say that, that a friend of mine from France came and saw my poem. And that's how my poem is in French as well. Because he translated it to French. I don't speak French. And when I made this poem poster, I felt it, it, it will give an extra message in this poem poster if I also have it in two languages, in French and English, that the message is universal, and that's a message in that. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this photograph is, is about-- is my father. His name is Tibor, T-I-B-O-R Fischl, F-I-S-C-H-L. And the place-- the place of this photograph was taken in Budapest, Hungary, in his early years. And that must have been the early 1900s, but I don't know its exact date. This photograph is my mother, Edith-- E-D-I-T-H Neubauer. And that looks like a picture was taken in her home prior to marrying my father in Budapest, Hungary. I would say that's at the end the 1800s. This is a family picture taken in Budapest around 1937. From left to right it's me, Peter Fischl. Next is my sister, Marika or Maria Fischl. Standing is my mother, Edith Fischl. And sitting is Tibor Fischl. And it's about 1937, Budapest, Hungary. My mother could have been here probably 20-- no, about 32 years old probably, and my father about maybe 40-some, mid-40s.

speaker: interviewer
text: The picture just went up.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Peter Fischl, me, at Kekesteto, a vacation place in Hungary in 1934. I was four years old. And this picture was taken before the picture you saw a few minutes ago where I was seven years old. This picture was taken in 1946 about-- I was 16 years old. This is Peter Fischl in Budapest, Hungary, at the-- at the Danube River. This is original paper I got at the Hungarian consulate to come to America. And that was official paper, the tag. It was on my coat. This is Alexander Wida, W-I-D-A, then husband of my sister Maria, Marika. And the picture was taken in about 1947. Alexander also not just saved my sister's life when he worked for the underground and also helped me to survive and got us the phony papers during the Holocaust, but he also escaped with me after the Hungarian Revolution. And he was a very brave person. When the communists were shooting at us with machine guns, he was the only one who didn't get up when they told everybody, if you don't get up, you're going to get killed. This is me and my son, Tibor Fischl, about 1965 at a friend's house here in Hollywood Hills, Los Angeles. On the left-hand side, that is my friend for 30-some years, Oscar-winning Ralph McQuarrie, artist-- motion picture artist. In the middle is my daughter, Cathy, C-A-T-H-Y, Fischl. And on the right-hand side, it's me, Peter Fischl, in Los Angeles. It was taken probably 10 years ago or so. This is the only thing I have left from the memory of my family and from the Holocaust, an object which I own. It's a flashlight from 1939 my father bought for me. At that time, it was an important thing. If the lights went out, that you could find your way around. Also it had filters in it so you could, you could put a red filter-- turn the front and a red filter would slide up, and the light wouldn't be so direct. And you could diffused. And then you could still use it during an air raid without getting the attention to yourself. And this is my real memory from, from everything.
